***The Leopard*** ([Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language"): *Il Gattopardo*, [lit.](/wiki/Literal_translation "Literal translation") 'The [Serval](/wiki/Serval "Serval")' [[il ˌɡattoˈpardo]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian"))[[3]](#cite_note-3) is a 1963 [epic](/wiki/Epic_film "Epic film") [historical drama](/wiki/Historical_drama "Historical drama") film directed by [Luchino Visconti](/wiki/Luchino_Visconti "Luchino Visconti"). Written by Visconti, [Suso Cecchi d'Amico](/wiki/Suso_Cecchi_d%27Amico "Suso Cecchi d'Amico"), [Enrico Medioli](/wiki/Enrico_Medioli "Enrico Medioli"), [Pasquale Festa Campanile](/wiki/Pasquale_Festa_Campanile "Pasquale Festa Campanile"), and [Massimo Franciosa](/wiki/Massimo_Franciosa "Massimo Franciosa"), the film is an [adaptation](/wiki/Film_adaptation "Film adaptation") of the [1958 novel](/wiki/The_Leopard "The Leopard") by [Giuseppe Tomasi di Lampedusa](/wiki/Giuseppe_Tomasi_di_Lampedusa "Giuseppe Tomasi di Lampedusa").[[4]](#cite_note-4)

[Burt Lancaster](/wiki/Burt_Lancaster "Burt Lancaster") stars as Don Fabrizio Corbera, an aging [Sicilian](/wiki/Sicilians "Sicilians") prince caught up in the sociopolitical turmoil of the *[Risorgimento](/wiki/Risorgimento "Risorgimento")* (Italian unification) during the mid-19th century, with [Alain Delon](/wiki/Alain_Delon "Alain Delon") as his opportunistic nephew Tancredi, and [Claudia Cardinale](/wiki/Claudia_Cardinale "Claudia Cardinale") as his goddaughter. [Paolo Stoppa](/wiki/Paolo_Stoppa "Paolo Stoppa"), [Rina Morelli](/wiki/Rina_Morelli "Rina Morelli"), [Romolo Valli](/wiki/Romolo_Valli "Romolo Valli"), [Serge Reggiani](/wiki/Serge_Reggiani "Serge Reggiani"), and [Terence Hill](/wiki/Terence_Hill "Terence Hill") play supporting roles. The film was an [international co-production](/wiki/International_co-production "International co-production") between Italian studio [Titanus](/wiki/Titanus "Titanus") and French studio [Pathé](/wiki/Path%C3%A9 "Pathé").[[5]](#cite_note-5)

The film won the [Palme d'Or](/wiki/Palme_d%27Or "Palme d'Or") at the [1963 Cannes Film Festival](/wiki/1963_Cannes_Film_Festival "1963 Cannes Film Festival"),[[6]](#cite_note-Ress_1963-6) and was released theatrically in Italy on March 28, 1963, and in France on June 14. It was a critical and commercial success in Europe, but reception was more lukewarm in the United States, where a truncated, English-dubbed cut was released. Retrospective reviews—of the film's longer original cut—have been more positive, and the film is now widely regarded as a classic and one of [the greatest movies ever made](/wiki/List_of_films_considered_the_best "List of films considered the best").[[7]](#cite_note-:1-7)[[8]](#cite_note-:2-8)

In 2008, the film was included on the [Italian Ministry of Cultural Heritage](/wiki/Ministry_of_Cultural_Heritage_and_Activities_and_Tourism "Ministry of Cultural Heritage and Activities and Tourism")'s [100 Italian films to be saved](/wiki/100_film_italiani_da_salvare "100 film italiani da salvare"), a list of 100 films that "have changed the collective memory of the country between 1942 and 1978."[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## Plot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Leopard_(1963_film)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Plot")]

In [Sicily](/wiki/Kingdom_of_the_Two_Sicilies "Kingdom of the Two Sicilies") in the year 1860, Don Fabrizio Corbera, Prince of [Salina](/wiki/The_Leopard#Locations "The Leopard"), enjoys the customary comforts and privileges of his ancestry. War has broken out between the armies of [Francis II of the Two Sicilies](/wiki/Francis_II_of_the_Two_Sicilies "Francis II of the Two Sicilies") and the insurgent volunteer [redshirts](/wiki/Redshirts_(Italy) "Redshirts (Italy)") of [Giuseppe Garibaldi](/wiki/Giuseppe_Garibaldi "Giuseppe Garibaldi"). Among the rebels is the Prince's nephew, Tancredi Falconeri, whose romantic politics the Prince hesitantly accepts with some whimsical sympathy. Upset by the uprising, the Prince departs to [Palermo](/wiki/Palermo "Palermo"). [Garibaldi's army](/wiki/Expedition_of_the_Thousand "Expedition of the Thousand") subjugates the city and expropriates Sicily from the [Bourbons](/wiki/House_of_Bourbon-Two_Sicilies "House of Bourbon-Two Sicilies"). The Prince muses upon the inevitability of change, with the middle class displacing the ruling class while on the surface everything remains the same. Refusing to bend to the tide of changes, the Prince departs to his summer palace at [Donnafugata](/wiki/The_Leopard#Locations "The Leopard").

A new national assembly calls a [plebiscite](/wiki/Referendum "Referendum") and the nationalists win 512–0, thanks to the corruption and support of the town's leading citizen, Don Calogero Sedara. Don Calogero is invited to the villa of the Corberas, and he brings his daughter Angelica with him. Both the Prince and Tancredi are taken by Angelica's beauty. Soon thereafter, Tancredi makes plans to ask for her hand in marriage. The Prince sees the wisdom of the match because he knows that, due to his nephew's vaulting ambition, Tancredi will be in need of ready cash, which Angelica's father will happily provide. With the blessing of both the Prince and Don Calogero, Tancredi and Angelica get engaged. Noticing Tancredi shift his allegiance from Garibaldi to [King Victor Emmanuel](/wiki/Victor_Emmanuel_II_of_Italy "Victor Emmanuel II of Italy")'s newly formed army, the Prince wistfully judges that his nephew is the kind of opportunist who will flourish in the new Italy.

Cavalier Chevalley, a representative from the [Kingdom of Sardinia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Sardinia_(1720%E2%80%931861) "Kingdom of Sardinia (1720–1861)"), comes to the Prince's villa. He pleads with the Prince to join the senate of the nascent [Kingdom of Italy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy "Kingdom of Italy"), hoping that the Prince's great compassion and wisdom will help alleviate the perceived poverty and alleged ignorance on the streets of Sicily. However, the Prince demurs and refuses this invitation, observing that, in his view, Sicily prefers its traditions to the delusions of modernity because its people are proud of their ancient heritage. He says he foresees his brand of aristocratic rulers, whom he refers to as leopards and lions, being replaced by more bureaucratic rulers, who he calls jackals and hyenas, but that everyone, including the "sheep" of the general public, will continue to see themselves as the "salt of the earth". The Prince recommends Don Calogero would make a more appropriate senator.

The Corberas, including Tancredi, attend a great ball at the villa of a neighbouring prince, and the event marks the debut of Angelica in [high society](/wiki/High_society "High society"). Afflicted by a combination of melancholia and the ridiculousness of the [nouveau riche](/wiki/Nouveau_riche "Nouveau riche"), the Prince wanders forlornly from room to room, increasingly disaffected by the entire edifice of the society he so gallantly represents—until Angelica approaches and asks him to dance. He accepts and, momentarily, recaptures and presents the elegant and dashing figure of his past self, but, after the dance, he quickly becomes disenchanted again.

In the early morning, the Prince leaves the ball alone. Walking with a heavy heart through the empty streets, he pauses to let a hurrying priest pass and enter a home to deliver [last rites](/wiki/Last_rites "Last rites"), and then walks into a dark alley that symbolises Italy's fading past, to which he feels he belongs.

## Production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Leopard_(1963_film)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Production")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/Palazzo_Gangi.jpg/250px-Palazzo_Gangi.jpg)](/wiki/File:Palazzo_Gangi.jpg)

The ballroom of Palazzo Valguarnera-Gangi, where the ballroom sequence was shot.

### Development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Leopard_(1963_film)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Development")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/Palermo%2C_Villa_Montalbo_Boscogrande.jpg/250px-Palermo%2C_Villa_Montalbo_Boscogrande.jpg)](/wiki/File:Palermo,_Villa_Montalbo_Boscogrande.jpg)

Villa Boscogrande, one of the film's primary locations.

[Tomasi](/wiki/Giuseppe_Tomasi_di_Lampedusa "Giuseppe Tomasi di Lampedusa")'s novel was a bestseller in Italy and won the [Strega Prize](/wiki/Strega_Prize "Strega Prize"), Italy's most prestigious literary award. In August 1960, Italian studio [Titanus](/wiki/Titanus "Titanus") announced they would make a film based on the novel in Sicily the following summer on a budget of at least $2 million. The film was to be an Italian-American co-production, shot in various languages, with a combination of Italian and American stars. [Ettore Giannini](/wiki/Ettore_Giannini "Ettore Giannini") was preparing a script, although it was expected he would collaborate with another writer to finish it.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Several treatments were reportedly done before Visconti became involved.[[11]](#cite_note-11) "The book is seen through the eyes of a Sicilian prince who has no sense of the people", said Visconti. "The people were fooled by Garibaldi and then they were destroyed by the Piedmontese. The popular conscience was strangled by the way the Piedmont upper class tried to keep the social structure of the south just as it was."[[12]](#cite_note-12)

In July 1961, MGM announced they had signed a co-production deal with Titanus to make the movie. [Warren Beatty](/wiki/Warren_Beatty "Warren Beatty") was in discussions with Visconti to play Tancredi, while Visconti approached [Laurence Olivier](/wiki/Laurence_Olivier "Laurence Olivier") and [Spencer Tracy](/wiki/Spencer_Tracy "Spencer Tracy") to play the Prince.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

Visconti was told by producers that they needed to cast a star in order to ensure they would earn enough money to justify the big budget. The producers recommended that the star should be either [Gregory Peck](/wiki/Gregory_Peck "Gregory Peck"), [Anthony Quinn](/wiki/Anthony_Quinn "Anthony Quinn"), [Spencer Tracy](/wiki/Spencer_Tracy "Spencer Tracy"), or Burt Lancaster, and chose Lancaster without consulting Visconti. This insulted the director, which caused tension on the set initially, but Visconti and Lancaster ended up working well together, and their resulting friendship lasted the rest of Visconti's life.[[14]](#cite_note-Buford_2000-14)

In November 1961, Lancaster agreed to play the lead role, with filming to start in April.[[15]](#cite_note-15) Lancaster said he had been "long fascinated" with *The Leopard*, even before being offered the role, saying: "I think it is the best written and most perceptive study of a man and his background that has appeared for many years."[[16]](#cite_note-burt-16) He said he had doubts about accepting the part because "the novel was so perfect as a novel", but, ultimately, decided to accept.[[17]](#cite_note-lan-17) Claudia Cardinale and Delon had made *Rocco and His Brothers* with Visconti.[[18]](#cite_note-claud-18)

In April 1962, 20th Century Fox announced it had bought the film's distribution rights.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

### Shooting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Leopard_(1963_film)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Shooting")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/Il_Gattopardo_%28Visconti%2C_1963%29_set_esterno_Croce_dei_Vespri.jpg/250px-Il_Gattopardo_%28Visconti%2C_1963%29_set_esterno_Croce_dei_Vespri.jpg)](/wiki/File:Il_Gattopardo_(Visconti,_1963)_set_esterno_Croce_dei_Vespri.jpg)

Visconti and Lancaster behind the scenes

Filming started in May 1962 in [Palermo](/wiki/Palermo "Palermo"). The first two weeks of the two-month location shoot in [Sicily](/wiki/Sicily "Sicily") were dedicated to battle scenes. After 22 weeks of location scenes, interiors were shot in Rome.[[20]](#cite_note-20) The film's climactic ball scene, which was shot in [Palazzo Valguarnera-Gangi](/wiki/Palazzo_Valguarnera-Gangi "Palazzo Valguarnera-Gangi") in Palermo, became famous for its duration (it runs for over 46 minutes in the completed film) and opulence.

Lancaster called Visconti "the finest director I've ever worked with."[[16]](#cite_note-burt-16) The scenes with Lancaster were shot in English and dubbed into Italian for the Italian version of the film, while most of the other scenes were filmed in Italian and dubbed into English for the English version.[[16]](#cite_note-burt-16) Lancaster was dubbed by [Corrado Gaipa](/wiki/Corrado_Gaipa "Corrado Gaipa"), and his French co-star [Alain Delon](/wiki/Alain_Delon "Alain Delon") was dubbed by [Carlo Sabatini](/wiki/Carlo_Sabatini_(actor) "Carlo Sabatini (actor)"). [Archibald Colquhoun](/wiki/Archibald_Colquhoun_(translator) "Archibald Colquhoun (translator)") worked as the film's dialogue director.[[21]](#cite_note-21) The French version was written by [René Barjavel](/wiki/Ren%C3%A9_Barjavel "René Barjavel").[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)

By May 1963, it was reported the film had cost Titanus $5 million.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

## Versions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Leopard_(1963_film)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Versions")]

*The Leopard* has circulated in at least five different versions. Visconti's initial [workprint](/wiki/Workprint "Workprint") was 205 minutes long, but both the director and producer felt it was too long, and the film was shortened to 195 minutes for its premiere at the Cannes Film Festival. Visconti then cut the film further—to 185 minutes—for its official Italian release, and considered this version to be his preferred one. In other European countries, the film was released in a 171-minute cut.

### English 1963 version

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Leopard_(1963_film)&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: English 1963 version")]

Twentieth Century-Fox distributed the movie in both England and the United States, making several significant alterations. They replaced Visconti's vivid [Technicolor](/wiki/Technicolor "Technicolor") images with the far inferior [DeLuxe Color](/wiki/DeLuxe_Color "DeLuxe Color") process and downgraded the film from 70mm to 35mm. Additionally, they cut several key scenes, further reducing the runtime from 185 to 161 minutes.[[25]](#cite_note-marcus-25)[[26]](#cite_note-nowell-26)[[27]](#cite_note-kehr-27)[[28]](#cite_note-nyt1983-28)

The dubbing was also poorly executed, with issues in the translation, the voices and the synchronization. In contrast, other Italian films of the time, such as those by [Riccardo Freda](/wiki/Riccardo_Freda "Riccardo Freda") and [Vittorio Cottafavi](/wiki/Vittorio_Cottafavi "Vittorio Cottafavi"), were carefully dubbed.[[25]](#cite_note-marcus-25)[[26]](#cite_note-nowell-26)[[27]](#cite_note-kehr-27) Burt Lancaster and supporting actor [Leslie French](/wiki/Leslie_French "Leslie French") were the only ones who re-dubbed their own dialogue.[[29]](#cite_note-sight-29)[[30]](#cite_note-skep-30) Critics deemed the dubbing as awful.[[28]](#cite_note-nyt1983-28)

The result of these changes was a significantly inferior product. As a consequence, the movie was poorly received by both critics and audiences in the US and England, unlike with the rest of Europe where it was well received.

Visconti himself had no control on any of these changes. He threatened to sue Fox, who threatened to counter-sue the director, arguing that Lancaster supervised the American cut.[[31]](#cite_note-31) "I don't feel it's my film at all," Visconti said of this version.[[32]](#cite_note-32) It is little surprise, therefore, that he has disavowed the English version of The Leopard and refuses to take any responsibility for it at all.[[26]](#cite_note-nowell-26)[[28]](#cite_note-nyt1983-28)

### English 1983 version

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Leopard_(1963_film)&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: English 1983 version")]

The 1983 print is a product of Fox's newly established 'classics division'. It is the 185-minute intermediate version approved by Visconti for international release. The Italian soundtrack has been restored, and the color has been enhanced to resemble the original Technicolor hues, which are now technically unreproducible. This version was received much more favorably by critics.[[27]](#cite_note-kehr-27)[[28]](#cite_note-nyt1983-28)

In 2004, a restored version was released by Fox.[[33]](#cite_note-offscreen-33)

## Release

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Leopard_(1963_film)&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Release")]

The film debuted at the [1963 Cannes Film Festival](/wiki/1963_Cannes_Film_Festival "1963 Cannes Film Festival"), where it won the [Palme d'Or](/wiki/Palme_d%27Or "Palme d'Or").[[6]](#cite_note-Ress_1963-6)

The 185-minute version of the film was finally released in the U.S. in 1983.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

### Preservation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Leopard_(1963_film)&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Preservation")]

The original 8-perforation [Technirama](/wiki/Technirama "Technirama") camera negative for *The Leopard* survives and was used by [The Criterion Collection](/wiki/The_Criterion_Collection "The Criterion Collection") to create their video master for DVD and Blu-ray, with [color timing](/wiki/Color_grading "Color grading") supervised by the film's cinematographer, [Giuseppe Rotunno](/wiki/Giuseppe_Rotunno "Giuseppe Rotunno"). New preservation film elements were created using a [4K](/wiki/4K_resolution "4K resolution") digital scan of the film, done with the cooperation of the [Cineteca di Bologna](/wiki/Cineteca_di_Bologna "Cineteca di Bologna"), [L'Immagine Ritrovata](/wiki/L%27Immagine_Ritrovata "L'Immagine Ritrovata"), [The Film Foundation](/wiki/The_Film_Foundation "The Film Foundation"), [Gucci](/wiki/Gucci "Gucci"), [Pathé](/wiki/Path%C3%A9 "Pathé"), Fondation Jérôme Seydoux-Pathé, [Twentieth Century Fox](/wiki/Twentieth_Century_Fox "Twentieth Century Fox"), and Centro Sperimentale di Cinematografia-Cineteca Nazionale.[[35]](#cite_note-fashionandrunway.com-35) This restoration premiered at the 2010 Cannes Film Festival.[[36]](#cite_note-Vanity_Fair-36)

### Home media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Leopard_(1963_film)&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Home media")]

There are several DVD editions of the film available:

* [Region 2](/wiki/DVD_region_code#Region_codes_and_countries "DVD region code") ([Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy")) The Medusa Home Entertainment release (released in 2001) contains the 185-minute Italian version with several bonus features and interviews. (This release is not English-friendly.)
* [Region 2](/wiki/DVD_region_code#Region_codes_and_countries "DVD region code") ([U.K.](/wiki/U.K. "U.K.")) The [BFI Video](/wiki/British_Film_Institute "British Film Institute") release offers a restored version of the Italian cut with an audio commentary by David Forgacs and Rossana Capitano.
* [Region 2](/wiki/DVD_region_code#Region_codes_and_countries "DVD region code") ([Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan")) The [Toho](/wiki/Toho "Toho") release contains an unrestored version of the Italian cut with both the original audio (and Japanese subtitles), and a rare alternative English dubbed track that is different from that of the shortened U.S. version of the film. Special features include text-based bios and facts in Japanese. (This release is not English-friendly.)
* [Region 1](/wiki/DVD_region_code#Region_codes_and_countries "DVD region code") ([U.S.](/wiki/U.S. "U.S.")) [The Criterion Collection](/wiki/The_Criterion_Collection "The Criterion Collection") release is a 3-disc set containing a restored version of the 185-minute Italian version (with optional English subtitles), several bonus features, interviews, an audio commentary by [Peter Cowie](/wiki/Peter_Cowie "Peter Cowie"), and the 161-minute U.S. English-dubbed version as an extra.

Blu-ray release:

* [Region A](/wiki/Blu-ray#A "Blu-ray") ([U.S.](/wiki/U.S. "U.S.")) [The Criterion Collection](/wiki/The_Criterion_Collection "The Criterion Collection") 2-disc Blu-ray set boasts a transfer of the 185-min Italian version in 1080P, most of the DVD bonus materials, some newly created bonus materials, and the 161-minute U.S. English-dubbed version in 1080i.

## Reception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Leopard_(1963_film)&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Reception")]

### Box office

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Leopard_(1963_film)&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Box office")]

The film was successful in Europe. It grossed $370,000 in its first ten days of release in eight Italian cities,[[37]](#cite_note-37) and was the sixth most popular film of the year at the French box office, with 3,688,024 admissions.[[2]](#cite_note-delon-2)[[38]](#cite_note-38) Due to the poor quality cut for U.S. release by Fox, the film did not perform as well in the United States, with [theatrical rentals](/wiki/Theatrical_rental "Theatrical rental") of $1.8 million.[[1]](#cite_note-rentals-1)[[30]](#cite_note-skep-30)

### Critical response

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Leopard_(1963_film)&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Critical response")]

*The Leopard* was well received in Europe, winning the 1963 Palme d'Or at Cannes and achieving both critical and commercial success.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

Due to Fox's poor English version, at the time of its release in the summer of 1963, the majority of American critics panned the film. According to *[Newsweek](/wiki/Newsweek "Newsweek")*, Lancaster looked "as if he's playing [Clarence Day](/wiki/Clarence_Day "Clarence Day")'s *[Life with Father](/wiki/Life_with_Father "Life with Father")* in summer stock."[[40]](#cite_note-Buford_2000_232-40) Jonathan Miller of *[The New Yorker](/wiki/The_New_Yorker "The New Yorker")* derided Lancaster as "muzzled by whiskers and clearly stunned by the importance of his role."[[40]](#cite_note-Buford_2000_232-40) However, *[Time Magazine](/wiki/Time_(magazine) "Time (magazine)")* praised Lancaster's characterization of the Leopard as solid and convincing.[[40]](#cite_note-Buford_2000_232-40)

The 1983 release, featuring its restored 185-minute runtime and Italian soundtrack, received a more favorable response from critics. *[New York](/wiki/New_York_(magazine) "New York (magazine)")* magazine called the now-famous ballroom scene "almost unbearably moving."[[41]](#cite_note-LLC1983-41) The *New York Times* wrote: "The reappearance of this enchanting work proves that, under the right circumstances, two decades make no difference whatsoever but 25 minutes can transform a very good film into a possibly great one."[[42]](#cite_note-42)[[33]](#cite_note-offscreen-33)

After seeing the 1983 re-release in full, critic for *[The New Yorker](/wiki/The_New_Yorker "The New Yorker")*, [Pauline Kael](/wiki/Pauline_Kael "Pauline Kael") wrote: "It's deeply satisfying to see [*The Leopard]* in its full length" and goes on to call it a "sweeping popular epic" and that it is "so beautifully felt that it calls up a whole culture. It casts an intelligent spell, intelligent and rapturous."[[43]](#cite_note-43)

After the 2004 release, the [Washington Post](/wiki/Washington_Post "Washington Post") praised the film, saying it 'was from a time “when epics looked like epics”, that “the movie all but weeps with a sense of emotional loss” and “we have the sublime satisfaction of a first-time experience with a yesteryear classic”.'[[33]](#cite_note-offscreen-33)[[44]](#cite_note-garrett-44)

### Retrospective reviews

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Leopard_(1963_film)&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Retrospective reviews")]

Mark Lager, on *[Senses of Cinema](/wiki/Senses_of_Cinema "Senses of Cinema")*, praised [Giuseppe Rotunno](/wiki/Giuseppe_Rotunno "Giuseppe Rotunno")'s cinematography in the film as "baroque and elegiac" and "groundbreaking and reflective".[[45]](#cite_note-45)

The film's reputation continues to rise. Directors [Martin Scorsese](/wiki/Martin_Scorsese "Martin Scorsese") and [Sydney Pollack](/wiki/Sydney_Pollack "Sydney Pollack") (who had supervised the English-dubbing of the film) consider it to be one of the greatest ever made.[[8]](#cite_note-:2-8)[[46]](#cite_note-:40-46) In the decennial poll of critics made by the [British Film Institute](/wiki/British_Film_Institute "British Film Institute"), *The Leopard* was named the 57th greatest film of all time.[[7]](#cite_note-:1-7)

On [review aggregator](/wiki/Review_aggregator "Review aggregator") website [Rotten Tomatoes](/wiki/Rotten_Tomatoes "Rotten Tomatoes"), the film holds an approval rating of 98% based on 50 reviews, with an average score of 9.1/10; the site's "critics consensus" reads: "Lavish and wistful, *The Leopard* features epic battles, sumptuous costumes, and a ballroom waltz that competes for most beautiful sequence committed to film."[[47]](#cite_note-47) On [Metacritic](/wiki/Metacritic "Metacritic"), the 2004 re-release of the film holds a perfect 100 out of 100 score based on 12 reviews, indicating "universal acclaim".[[48]](#cite_note-48)

The film was included by the Vatican in [a list of important films](/wiki/Vatican%27s_list_of_films "Vatican's list of films") compiled in 1995, under the category of "Art".[[49]](#cite_note-49)

Main article: [The Leopard (TV series)](/wiki/The_Leopard_(TV_series) "The Leopard (TV series)")

A 5-part streaming television [miniseries](/wiki/Miniseries "Miniseries") based on the novel, was released on Netflix on 5 March 2025.[[51]](#cite_note-51)
